source: Kabat-Zinn, J. (1990). Full Catastrophe Living: Using the Wisdom of Your Body and Mind to Face Stress, Pain, and Illness.
description: Evidence-based mindfulness meditation practices from the MBSR (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction) tradition and related approaches. These practices cultivate present-moment awareness, non-judgmental acceptance, and compassionate attention.

strategies.name: Body Scan Meditation
strategies.description: Systematically bring awareness to each part of the body, cultivating present-moment awareness and releasing physical tension.
steps: Lie down on your back or sit comfortably
Close your eyes and take three deep breaths
Bring attention to your left foot - notice any sensations without trying to change them
Breathe into the left foot, imagining breath flowing to and from that area
Move attention to left ankle, calf, knee, thigh - spending 30 seconds with each
Repeat with right leg
Continue through: pelvis, lower back, abdomen, chest, upper back, shoulders
Move through left arm: hand, forearm, elbow, upper arm, shoulder
Repeat with right arm
Scan through: neck, jaw, face, back of head, top of head
Expand awareness to the whole body as one complete, breathing organism
Rest in this full-body awareness for several minutes
When ready, gently wiggle fingers and toes, slowly open eyes

citations.author: Goldberg et al.
citations.title: Mindfulness-based interventions for psychiatric disorders: A systematic review and meta-analysis

strategies.contraindications: May be uncomfortable initially; some people find it difficult to lie still

strategies.name: Loving-Kindness Meditation (Metta)
strategies.description: Cultivate feelings of goodwill, kindness, and warmth toward self and others.
steps: Sit comfortably, close your eyes, take a few deep breaths
Begin with yourself: 'May I be safe. May I be healthy. May I be happy. May I live with ease.'
Repeat these phrases slowly, letting the meaning sink in
Notice any warmth, resistance, or other feelings that arise
Bring to mind someone you love: picture them, feel their presence
Offer them the phrases: 'May you be safe. May you be healthy. May you be happy. May you live with ease.'
Next, a neutral person (someone you see but don't know well)
Offer them the same phrases with genuine goodwill
Then, if ready, a difficult person (start with mildly difficult)
Extend the phrases to them, noticing any resistance
Finally, expand to all beings: 'May all beings be safe, healthy, happy, and live with ease'
Rest in the feeling of universal kindness
Gently return attention to your breath and slowly open eyes

citations.author: Salzberg, S.
citations.title: Lovingkindness: The Revolutionary Art of Happiness

citations.author: Hofmann et al.
citations.title: Loving-Kindness and Compassion Meditation: Potential for Psychological Interventions

strategies.contraindications: Can be emotionally challenging if self-compassion is difficult; may bring up grief or loss

strategies.name: RAIN Practice (Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture)
strategies.description: Mindful approach to working with difficult emotions, developed by Michele McDonald and Tara Brach.
steps: R - RECOGNIZE: Name what you're experiencing - 'This is anxiety' or 'This is hurt'
Acknowledge it's present without trying to fix it
A - ALLOW: Let the experience be there. Say 'yes' to what is
Stop fighting, resisting, or trying to make it different
You don't have to like it, just allow it to be present
I - INVESTIGATE: Get curious about the experience with kindness
Where do you feel it in your body? What's the dominant sensation?
What beliefs or stories come with this feeling?
What does this vulnerable place need?
N - NURTURE: Offer yourself compassion and care
Place hand on heart or other comforting gesture
Say what you need to hear: 'It's okay', 'I'm here', 'This is hard'
Notice any shift in the experience after completing RAIN

citations.author: Brach, T.
citations.title: True Refuge: Finding Peace and Freedom in Your Own Awakened Heart

strategies.contraindications: May be intense with severe trauma; proceed gently or with therapist support

strategies.name: Mindful Noting Practice
strategies.description: Label experiences as they arise to maintain present-moment awareness and reduce reactivity.
steps: Sit comfortably and close your eyes
Begin with attention on the breath
When something pulls your attention away, silently note it
For thoughts: 'thinking', 'planning', 'worrying', 'remembering'
For sensations: 'tingling', 'pressure', 'warmth', 'tension'
For emotions: 'anxiety', 'sadness', 'joy', 'irritation'
For sounds: 'hearing'
Note gently and return attention to breath
The note should be light - like touching a bubble without popping it
Don't analyze or elaborate - just note and return
If lost in thought for a while, that's okay - note 'thinking' and return
Practice for 10-20 minutes

citations.author: Goldstein, J.
citations.title: Mindfulness: A Practical Guide to Awakening

strategies.name: Choiceless Awareness
strategies.description: Open, panoramic awareness without focusing on any particular object - letting experience unfold naturally.
steps: Sit comfortably, eyes closed or soft gaze
Begin with several minutes of breath awareness to settle
Now release the focus on breath
Open your awareness to receive whatever arises
Don't go looking for experiences - let them come to you
Sounds, sensations, thoughts, emotions - all welcome
Notice whatever is most prominent in awareness
When something becomes strong, let awareness rest there
When it fades, return to open, receptive awareness
You're not controlling attention - you're allowing it to be drawn naturally
Like sitting by a window watching the street - you don't make things happen
Practice this spacious, allowing awareness
If you get lost, return briefly to breath, then open again

citations.author: Goldstein, J.
citations.title: Mindfulness: A Practical Guide to Awakening

citations.author: Kabat-Zinn, J.
citations.title: Wherever You Go, There You Are

strategies.contraindications: Advanced practice; start with more structured meditations first

strategies.name: Mountain Meditation
strategies.description: Cultivate qualities of stability, groundedness, and equanimity through mountain imagery.
steps: Sit in a stable, dignified posture
Close your eyes and take several deep breaths
Visualize a mountain - any mountain that speaks to you
See its base rooted deep in the earth
Notice its peak reaching toward the sky
The mountain sits with immovable stability through all seasons
In spring, new life emerges on its slopes
In summer, it basks in warmth and light
In fall, leaves change and fall - the mountain remains
In winter, it's buried in snow and ice - still the mountain sits
Storms rage, winds blow, weather changes - the mountain endures
Now bring that mountain into your body
Your head is the peak, your base solid and grounded
Become the mountain - dignified, rooted, enduring
Thoughts and emotions are like weather passing through
You remain - solid, stable, unshakeable essence
Rest in mountain awareness for 10-15 minutes

citations.author: Kabat-Zinn, J.
citations.title: Wherever You Go, There You Are

strategies.name: Walking Meditation
strategies.description: Bring mindful awareness to the simple act of walking, coordinating body and mind.
steps: Find a path 10-20 paces long where you can walk back and forth
Stand at one end, feet hip-width apart, hands clasped in front or behind
Bring awareness to your whole body standing
Feel the contact of feet on ground, weight distribution
Begin walking very slowly
Note each component: 'lifting', 'moving', 'placing' the foot
Keep attention on the physical sensations of walking
When you reach the end, pause, turn mindfully, pause again
Continue walking back and forth
If mind wanders, gently bring attention back to the feet
You can walk at natural pace, noting 'left', 'right'
Or very slowly, noting each micro-movement
Practice for 10-30 minutes
End by standing still, noticing the stillness
strategies.contraindications: Requires space to walk; may not be suitable for all mobility levels

meta.evidence_base: Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) and Mindfulness-Based Cognitive Therapy (MBCT) are supported by extensive research showing effectiveness for stress, anxiety, depression, chronic pain, and general well-being. Meta-analyses show medium to large effect sizes.
meta.target_populations: Widely applicable across clinical and non-clinical populations. Particularly effective for stress-related conditions, anxiety, depression, chronic pain, and those seeking to cultivate greater present-moment awareness and emotional regulation.
meta.core_principles: Non-judgmental awareness, present-moment focus, acceptance of what is, beginner's mind, non-striving, letting go, trust in one's own experience.
meta.training_note: While these practices can be learned independently, 8-week MBSR or MBCT programs provide structured learning with group support. Regular daily practice (even 10-15 minutes) is more beneficial than occasional longer sessions.
meta.progression: Start with Body Scan or Walking Meditation (easier, concrete anchor). Progress to Noting and RAIN (intermediate). Advanced practitioners can explore Choiceless Awareness and Loving-Kindness.
